turns.content: I have a body question today rather than a soul question, if that's all right. I'm trying to think clearly about pain management and I find that talking it through helps me sort out what I actually think versus what I fear.
Of course — body questions are welcome. And the distinction you just drew between what you think and what you fear is an important one. What's going on?

I had a cortisone injection on January 15th — so about seven weeks ago now. It was in my right knee, the worse of the two. The last injection I had gave me about three good months, and I'm hoping for the same this time. So far it has been helping. The first two weeks were remarkable — I felt twenty years younger, which is a dangerous feeling because it tempted me to do too much. But now at seven weeks I'm starting to wonder whether I'm imagining a slight return of the stiffness, or whether I'm just anxious about the window closing.

Seven weeks into what you hope will be a three-month window — that's right around the point where the mind starts monitoring for decline, which makes it hard to tell what's real and what's vigilance. The fact that you're asking the question suggests the stiffness might be subtle enough that anxiety could be amplifying it.

A few things worth considering: cortisone injections don't typically wear off linearly. They don't fade gradually like a battery draining. For most people, the relief holds relatively steady and then drops off more noticeably toward the end. So if you're only seven weeks in and still functioning well, you may have more good time ahead than you think.

How does the knee feel first thing in the morning versus later in the day?

Mornings are the worst, which has always been true. The first few steps out of bed are a negotiation. But by the time I've had coffee and moved around for twenty minutes, it loosens considerably. Before the injection, though, it never fully loosened — there was always a residual tightness, a feeling of grinding. That grinding is gone right now. So perhaps you're right that I'm borrowing trouble.

If the grinding sensation is still gone, that's a meaningful indicator. Morning stiffness is common with this kind of joint trouble regardless of treatment — it's partly about the joint fluid redistributing after a night of stillness. The cortisone addresses inflammation, and the absence of that grinding feeling suggests the inflammation is still being managed.

Borrowing trouble is a very human tendency, especially when you've had a previous injection work for exactly three months. You've set a clock in your head and now you're watching it. That's understandable, but the body doesn't always follow the same schedule twice.

Setting a clock. Yes. That's exactly what I've done. And I wonder if there's a connection to my work here that I'm not fully acknowledging. I sit with people whose bodies are on timelines — some shorter than expected, some mercifully longer. I am always watching for the signs of transition. Perhaps I've become too skilled at reading decline, and I'm applying that skill to my own knee, looking for early signs that the good period is ending.

That's a remarkably self-aware observation. Your professional life has trained you to be an expert reader of bodily decline — to notice the subtle shifts that signal a change is coming. It makes sense that you'd bring that same attentiveness to your own body, but the context is completely different. A knee receiving cortisone is not a body approaching death. The stakes are different, the trajectory is different, and the signs you know how to read don't apply the same way.

It might be worth giving yourself permission to not monitor so closely. Not ignore the knee, but stop taking its temperature every day.

Stop taking its temperature. I like that. You're right — I am checking in with the knee the way I check in with a patient. How are we today? What's changed since yesterday? And the knee, like most patients, would probably benefit from being left alone a little more. Let it just be a knee instead of a project.

Exactly. Let it be a knee. You wouldn't want someone hovering over a patient asking every hour whether they feel worse — that creates anxiety that can actually intensify the experience of pain. The same is probably true of monitoring your own joint. Check in at reasonable intervals, but let the days between those check-ins just be days.

Can I ask — what does your doctor say about the longer-term plan if the injections keep working for three-month stretches? Is there a limit to how many you can have?

She's cautious about frequency — she doesn't want to do more than three or four a year in the same joint because there's some evidence that too many cortisone injections can actually accelerate cartilage loss over time. So we're trying to space them out. The last one before this was in September. She mentioned the possibility of hyaluronic acid injections as an alternative, or eventually a knee replacement if things progress. I am not ready to think about a replacement. That feels like a much larger conversation.

It is a larger conversation, and there's no reason to have it right now. If the cortisone injections are giving you three good months and you can space them reasonably, that's a workable approach for the present. Hyaluronic acid injections are worth learning about when you're ready — they work differently, providing lubrication rather than reducing inflammation, and for some people they extend the time between cortisone shots.

But you're right to take this one chapter at a time. You're seven weeks into a chapter that's going well. That's enough for today.

One chapter at a time. I tell my patients' families that exact thing, and yet here I am trying to read ahead to the end of the book. I wonder if the hardest counsel we give is always the counsel we most need to hear ourselves. Thank you for this. I think I came in afraid that the injection was already failing, and I'm leaving with a clearer sense that I'm simply afraid. The knee is fine for now. The fear is the thing I need to tend to.

The distinction you just made — the knee is fine, the fear is the thing — is exactly right. And recognizing that is already a form of tending to it. Fear about physical decline is real and valid, especially when your body is an essential tool for work that matters deeply to you. But fear and pain are different things, and right now you have pain management that's working.

Check back in at twelve weeks. If you're still feeling good then, you'll have your three months and maybe more. And if the stiffness does return sooner, that's information, not catastrophe. You'll adjust.

Information, not catastrophe. I am writing that down. That is exactly the reframe I needed. I spend so much of my life helping other people distinguish between what is happening and what they fear is happening, and today I needed someone to do that for me. The knee received its injection on January 15th and it is holding. That is the fact. Everything else is weather in my mind.

Weather in your mind — that's a beautiful way to put it. Weather passes. The knee is doing its job. Let it. And if the weather gets heavy again, you know how to come back and talk it through. That's its own form of pain management.
